locked_facts: Carnot efficiency: η = 1 - T_cold/T_hot (temperatures in Kelvin)
No real engine can exceed Carnot efficiency
η < 1 always (because T_cold can never be absolute zero)
Heat Q_C must always be rejected to cold reservoir
Carnot cycle: two isothermal + two adiabatic processes
truth_statements: The Carnot engine is the most efficient engine operating between two given temperatures
No engine can have efficiency greater than the Carnot engine for the same temperature limits
The efficiency of a Carnot engine depends only on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs
A heat engine must reject some heat to the cold reservoir — complete conversion to work is impossible
The Carnot cycle consists of two reversible isothermal and two reversible adiabatic processes

aha_moment.what_to_show: Efficiency calculation: η = 1 - 300/600 = 50%. Even this PERFECT engine wastes 50% of heat! Arrow diagram: Q_H=1000J enters, W=500J exits as work, Q_C=500J MUST be rejected. PV diagram shows complete Carnot cycle (rectangle-ish with isotherms and adiabats).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Large efficiency number (50%) glowing. Q_C arrow to cold reservoir is thick and unavoidable. No matter how perfect the engine, it CANNOT convert all heat to work.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that even the theoretical best engine (zero friction, perfectly reversible) MUST reject heat. 100% efficiency is thermodynamically impossible, not just an engineering limitation.

simulation_states.label: The setup — hot and cold reservoirs
simulation_states.description: Hot reservoir (red, T_H=600K) at top, cold reservoir (blue, T_C=300K) at bottom. Engine block in the middle. No arrows yet — just the structure.
simulation_states.emphasis: Two temperature reservoirs define the maximum possible efficiency. Everything depends on T_H and T_C.

simulation_states.label: Heat flows and work output
simulation_states.description: Q_H = 1000J flows from hot reservoir to engine (red arrow). W = 500J exits engine to the right (green arrow). Q_C = 500J flows from engine to cold reservoir (blue arrow).
simulation_states.emphasis: Energy conservation: Q_H = W + Q_C. The engine converts PART of the heat to work. The rest MUST go to the cold reservoir.

simulation_states.label: Efficiency limit — even perfection has limits
simulation_states.description: Efficiency η = 1 - T_C/T_H = 1 - 300/600 = 50% displayed prominently. Even this ideal (frictionless, reversible) engine wastes half the heat. PV diagram shows complete Carnot cycle.
simulation_states.emphasis: AHA: This is the MAXIMUM possible efficiency. No real engine can do better. And it is only 50%! You CANNOT make it 100% unless T_C = 0 K (impossible).

simulation_states.label: Explore — what changes efficiency?
simulation_states.description: All elements visible. PV diagram shows Carnot cycle. Efficiency formula. Arrow diagram. Caption: To increase η, raise T_H or lower T_C. But T_C > 0 always, so η < 100% always.
simulation_states.emphasis: The second law of thermodynamics: complete conversion of heat to work is impossible. Every engine must reject some heat.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A perfect frictionless engine can achieve 100% efficiency
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Even a perfectly frictionless, reversible (Carnot) engine cannot achieve 100% efficiency. The Carnot efficiency η = 1 - T_C/T_H is ALWAYS less than 1 because the cold reservoir temperature T_C is always greater than absolute zero (0 K). This is a fundamental thermodynamic limit, not an engineering limitation. The second law of thermodynamics forbids complete conversion of heat to work in a cyclic process.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the ideal Carnot engine with η = 50%. Thick blue arrow (Q_C) shows heat MUST be rejected. Even with zero friction, half the heat is wasted. This is physics, not engineering.

trigger_keywords: gas change karo toh efficiency badhegi
efficiency gas type pe depend karti hai
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Carnot efficiency depends on the working substance (type of gas)
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Carnot efficiency depends ONLY on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs: η = 1 - T_C/T_H. It does not depend on the type of gas, amount of gas, or any property of the working substance. This is one of the most remarkable results in thermodynamics.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the formula η = 1 - T_C/T_H with only temperature values. No gas properties appear in the formula. The efficiency is purely determined by reservoir temperatures.

typical_question_types: calculate Carnot efficiency for given T_H and T_C
find Q_C given Q_H and efficiency
compare efficiencies of engines with different temperature limits
prove no engine can exceed Carnot efficiency
common_traps: using Celsius instead of Kelvin in efficiency formula
thinking efficiency can reach 100% with ideal conditions
confusing COP of refrigerator with engine efficiency
forgetting Q_C must always be positive
